content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Many web developers spend most of their time inside application code.
Components, APIs, databases, authentication, forms, tests, deployments — these are familiar. But the moment something goes wrong with DNS, CDN caching, SSL certificates, or edge routing, the whole system can suddenly feel mysterious.
The website is deployed, but the domain does not work. The API works locally, but production returns CORS errors. A new page is released, but users still see the old one. SSL is valid in one region and broken in another. A redirect works in staging but loops in production.
These problems are frustrating because they happen outside the code you usually look at.
But the mental model does not have to be complicated.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Understanding the request path makes infrastructure problems much easier to debug.When a user opens a website, the browser first needs to find where the domain points. That is DNS. DNS does not serve your app. It tells the browser which server, CDN, or edge network should receive the request.
After DNS resolves, the request often reaches a CDN or edge network. This layer may handle SSL, redirects, caching, compression, static assets, and routing. In many modern apps, the edge layer is the first real entry point into the production system.
Then the request may go to a frontend server, serverless function, API gateway, or backend service. That service may call databases, queues, third-party APIs, payment providers, or internal services.
When something fails, it helps to know which layer is responsible.
A DNS issue usually means the browser cannot reliably find the right destination. A CDN issue may mean users receive cached content, wrong redirects, blocked assets, or inconsistent regional behavior. An application server issue may produce 500 errors, timeouts, or broken API responses. A database issue may make the app slow even though routing is correct.
One useful debugging habit is to follow the request step by step instead of guessing.
Domain resolves?
SSL certificate valid?
CDN route correct?
Cache behaving as expected?
Frontend server responding?
API responding?
Database healthy?
Third-party services available?This sounds simple, but it prevents panic. Infrastructure problems often feel huge because every layer is invisible at once. Breaking the path into layers makes the issue smaller.
CDN caching is one of the most common sources of confusion. Caching is powerful because it makes websites faster and reduces load on origin servers. But stale caches can make deployments look broken. A file may be updated on the server while the CDN still serves the old version.
This is why cache headers matter.
Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutableThis kind of header is great for hashed static assets because the filename changes when the content changes.
app.8f3a1c.js
styles.91ab2.cssBut HTML documents usually need a different strategy, because they reference the latest assets.
Cache-Control: no-cachePress enter or click to view image in full size

Good cache strategy treats HTML and hashed assets differently.Edge routing can also create surprises. Many apps use redirects from www to root domains, HTTP to HTTPS, old paths to new paths, or region-based routing. A small misconfiguration can create redirect loops.
For example, one layer may redirect HTTP to HTTPS while another layer thinks the original request was HTTP because of a missing proxy header. The app keeps redirecting even though the user is already on HTTPS. These problems are easier to debug when headers and proxy behavior are understood.
Web developers do not need to become full-time infrastructure engineers to understand this. But knowing the request path makes production issues much less scary. When a user says the site is down, you can ask better questions. Is DNS resolving? Is the CDN serving the request? Is the API failing? Is the issue global or regional? Is it cached? Is it only one browser?
The browser does not care which team owns which layer. It only knows whether the request works.
That is why frontend, backend, and infrastructure understanding increasingly overlap. Modern web developers benefit from knowing how the app reaches users, not just how the code works after it runs.
A clean mental model of DNS, CDN, and edge routing can save hours of confusion during incidents. It turns “production is broken” into a sequence of specific checks.
And specific checks are always better than panic.
Further reading: To dive deeper into DNS routing, CDN caching, edge deployment, SSL debugging, and production infrastructure safety, read my latest technical articles on Medium and join the engineering community on Substack. I share practical system breakdowns there every week.
